sentences.text: Yes, as a matter of fact, other children did join because it made it more interesting.
The group that I was with, there were four of us, you see?
She actually took my friends, you see, so that, first of all, it created a wonderful atmosphere, and there was the old competition, you see?
I was-- I-- it might not be nice to say-- sounds like bragging-- but I was very, very good student in-- in my childhood and after.
But anyhow, I loved school.
I absolutely adored school.
I never had enough of it, you see?
So that these children were more or less on my level, and the old competitive spirit was there, you see?
So they-- they were actual lessons with homework, with-- you know, divided into units of-- of-- you know, this is for a year, for this year, for fifth year, for sixth year, whichever.
You see?
It made it extremely interesting, really.
And I personally, speaking of that, with a neighbor's boy who was my friend-- he was not in my class-- we decided to open a library, a lending library.
[LAUGHS] He reminds me of it all the time.
I see him once a year when I'm in New York when we meet for the yahrzeit.
You know, once a year, my hometown survivors meet in New York to-- for the-- for the anniversary of the liquidation of town.
And he-- he lives in Connecticut.
He comes in-- [?
Mitye. ?] And he always reminds me.
He says, remember when he opened the library?
We really opened the lending library.
And the kids who were not attending, quote unquote 'my class,' you see, we had a little stand, and we had books, and we wrote out cards.
I-- funny thing is that years and years later when I-- my kids were little and I worked as a volunteer in the Children's Library for 11 years.
[LAUGHS]
And I was always very proud of that.
He always reminds me of it.
Once a year, we see each other, we talk about it.
No, it wasn't secretive.
I told you, the ghetto was an open ghetto, and the children had to find a way of amusing themselves, because everything that was once part of our life was now closed.
Everything, including the libraries, however-- whatever it was.
Well, yes, because there was correspondence.
You know, my parents always wrote a lot of letters, and we heard from [INAUDIBLE].
We heard from my father's family in [INAUDIBLE], and so on.
But war was war and people just-- you know, I should not be an authority on it.
But as far as I can tell now, I really was not aware of the true-- [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] the extent of what this was going to be about.
All I know is those things that I did before could not be done anymore.

And being so protected by our parents who didn't want us to know certain things, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I was really, truly amazed what came-- the things that came later, that-- that this unspeakable horror that came later, with the camps and the-- concentration camps and the chimneys.
But of course, that was later on.
But at that point, while we lived in the ghetto, life was as close to normal, except you had to know never to go out when the Germans were out with the dogs.
Absolutely never.
And if you saw a German wherever, three blocks down the street, you had to hide right away because they would let the dog at you or whatever.
So--
Yes.
No.
Not me.
I know that these lovely friends-- you know, I was the youngest and I was still that sort of child.
I wasn't really included in the poetry readings.
I just sat there, listening and hearing.
But I was not part of the so-called [INAUDIBLE] Youth.
Like, they were called the Golden Youth, you see.
I wasn't part of the Golden Youth.
But everybody took it, I imagine, as a sign of things changing during the war.
Populations being resettled or however.
But I can assure you, even though I really don't know that for a fact, nobody ever could have expected what really came.
You know, what happened in the other war, they didn't have bombs.
Well, this war, they had bombs, you see.
The last war, whatever they had or didn't have, this was a little different.
So you sort of allowed yourself to account for it.
Not myself, but I can imagine, you know, the shifts of population and such.
But nobody, I can assure you, would ever have thought that the final-- the final point in that progression would be the final solution, which is obliterating a whole nation.
And that is the point.

So that I really don't think-- well, these people from Lodz ghetto, they came in 1941, you see, so there wasn't yet-- The real final solution that was signed, sealed, and delivered was in 1942 in the Wannsee-- at the Wannsee Conference.
So, you see, prior to that, you could still rationalize that this was just population shifting or whatever it was.
Well, after we were thrown out of our apart-- our first apartment, we were in three different apartments.
And the last, the third one were the-- with the teamsters.
That's where the final solution found us.
The-- the clearing of the town of Jews found us with the teamster.
Yes.
This-- obviously.

Before I tell you this, I have to explain to you that already, at that time-- and this is 1942, in the summer-- the word got out that if you had a job, that there were going to be big changes because people are going to be picked for jobs.
If you had a job, you could really save your life.
Well, my two sisters found a job in a brick factory, not in that munitions factory but in a brick factory.
And those were also three shifts.
It so happened that the night-- the morning when the Gestapo and the Latvians came to get us out of the apartment, my sisters were still at work.
You see?
They were kept there.
They were not released after the night shift.
So that I was that alone with my parents in the house when we heard that knock on the door and with the butts of the rifles.
The Latvians were in uniform.
Those were the collaborating Latvians.
And everyone, every family, was pushed and-- and hurried toward the central square of the town.
And I was walking with my mother and father.
Of course, we had no idea whether we'll find my sisters there or whatever.
They just went with us.
And the horror on that-- it wasn't such a big walk.
I mean, probably no more than a mile from where we were to the central square.
They were shooting at people.
They were smashing babies against the wall, you know?
I have never really seen a dead person, even during the evac-- during-- when the war broke out, when we were in the forest and all.
If I saw a dead horse on the road, I covered my eyes.

I just-- and here, not only did I see dead people, but I saw people falling in front of me, and children snatched and-- and thrown against-- against the wall and old people who couldn't wait-- who couldn't walk would be hit with the butts and then shot.
Anyhow, on the way, we passed a-- one of the buildings where my mother's elderly woman friend lived.
She-- she couldn't walk, and she was-- one of her daughters was in Palestine, you see?
My mother was very friendly with her.
And mother-- her name was Mrs.
Kazimerska. ?] And Mother took her under the arm to help her out, and Father held me by the hand, and we all went to the square.
And at the square, there were all these people and children, and everybody was crying.
And the religious Jews were praying and all that.
And the Gestapo was there, and they were pointing.
This way, this way, this way, this way.
My father was pointed that way, where the men were.
I was pointed this way.
And my mother and Mrs.
Kazimerska ?] was pointed another way.
And nobody knew which way is which because, I mean, it was-- it was just-- you know, as they were pushing the people into the square, they were lining them up in groups for marching.
And it was-- this was October, 1942. 27th of October.
And [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] we were now marched in the direction of the factory.
We meaning myself.
I really didn't know what-- about my mother or my father.
And I didn't know about my sisters.
But there are very kind people.
You know, I-- I am always saying this, and I will forever say, that I really survived because people were very-- always very kind to me.
You know?
Somehow, people always took care of me.
I don't know why.
Probably didn't deserve it.
But one way or another, people somehow went out of their way so to help me.
Well anyhow, I found myself in that group, marching, and the ladies-- I was terrified.
I was absolutely beside myself because here I was, all alone, left not knowing who was were.
And my mother made-- I was skinny and I was-- you know, I looked very young.
And Mother put rouge on me, and she stuffed-- she put something like-- something like a brassiere, to make me look that I was grown up, which I-- I was like a board, flat board.
I was a child.
Anyhow, so I find myself here stuffed full of rags to fill me out.
It was very warm.
The excitement, the dogs, the barking dogs.
They would send the dogs on the people who wouldn't march in line.
They would shoot at anybody who didn't stay in.
And of course, whoever couldn't walk, they-- forget it.
Then it was-- they were shot immediately.
Anyhow, soon after this march was over, which was horrendous, I found myself in a camp not far from that factory, that munitions factory.
I did not know that place.
That place was once called-- was a firing range.
And that's why the Polish name for it is Strzelnica.
Strzelnica is a firing range, you see?
This Strzelnica was actually converted.
There was some barracks put in or whatever.
Or maybe there were barracks there before, but I really don't know.
Because this was not anymore in the town.
This-- the community in which I lived had this industrial town and a bedroom town, you see?
The bedroom town where everybody lived was called Wierzbnik.
And the big town, the industrial part where the factory was, was Starachowice.
It was really combined and named Starachowice, but everybody lived in the bedroom town.
Now, this Strzelnica camp was very near the factory.
I have never heard of it, I've never seen it, and I found myself completely alone.
I cried and I was petrified.
I think the terror-- the real terror that gripped me in all my war-- war experiences, that was that point.
Was of the greatest terror in my life because I was really alone, totally alone, for the first time in my life.
And it was only thanks to the people around me who comforted me, who-- who hugged me, who held me by my hand, and-- and don't cry, and your mother will come soon, and all this.
But I was totally stricken with horror, with-- with fear.
I mean, I have experienced fear after this point, too, you know, with the [? write-through. ?] After all, the war was already on for two years.
So I have experienced enough fear.
But nothing like that excruciating pain.
Well, after everything settled down, I discovered that my father found himself in the same camp where I was.
And we also found out that there was another camp established around the area in a place called Majowka.
And we found out subsequently that my two sisters, who were fin-- they were finally released after the town was evacuated-- cleared of Jews-- they found themselves in that other camp.
But meanwhile, we also discovered that people who did not go to either Majowka or Strzelnica camp were taken away to the railroad, loaded onto trains, and sent somewhere.
We did not know.
But of course, now we know that this was Treblinka.
But at that point, there was talk about resettlement.
You know, the euphemism forever being used that these people were going to work here in the factory, and the others were going to be resettled.
You see?
But the trains that left that day where my mother with Mrs.
Kazimerska ?] were among those, that went straight to Treblinka.
And we know it for a fact now.
And as a matter of fact, in Treblinka, there is-- you know, they have these jagged stones for each Jewish community.
There is one big stone with a sign Starachowice, because we were, I think, among the early-- our town was among the early camp-- communities that was liquidated and sent to Treblinka.
Very-- very soon.
I'll tell you why.
Because, you see, there were men and there were women, and every-- we were separated.
But everybody, you know, found out who was among the men and who was among the women.
This was-- of course, we didn't live in the same barracks but that didn't matter.
You already-- I already saw.
And my father, again, became a scribe.
My father was given-- he was typing up whatever it was.
He was typing their schedules, whatever.
And most of these people were sent to work in the factory.
I worked in the munitions factory myself, but for a very short time because, subsequently, I was assigned to the laundry, to do laundry.
And if I tell you what laundry means, you would probably change your mind about what laundry is all about.
But anyhow, the munitions factory was producing cannon for armaments.
And my job was to put the cannon on some kind of a machine that turned, and to set a little knife to it, and the knife would strip off the first strip.
You know, like-- like peeling a sausage type.
You know?
And it was-- this cannon turned like a sausage, and the knife was cutting it and cutting it.
And when the cutter stopped, you took off that cannon.
It wasn't a big one.
It was that size.
You took it off.
You put it on a pile.
You put another cannon.
And of course, we had supervisors and others.
And they beat us if anything wasn't done right.
And in fact, you know, a piece-- this was very hot metal, you understand?
This strip, it's like an apple.
You know when you peel an apple, and the thing goes and goes and goes, the skin?
Well, this was analogous.
It was that way.
And at one time, a piece of that hot metal, that-- that strip, burst and it fell in here.
So to this day, I have a scar here and in between my toes.
To this day, this is my-- the-- this is the souvenir I took out of that factory.
But people worked extremely hard because the men were attending the smelting oven.
This is one horrendous job.
You know, this is an open hearth, open-- I don't know what the way you call it.
Open pit?
It wasn't a pit.
But you know when iron ore is smelted.
To tell you what-- how many degrees of heat comes out of that, I couldn't tell you.
I mean, I just don't know these things.
But we were on the same premises, you know?
The-- the-- the cannon stripping was adjacent to that smelting oven, whatever you called it.
And this is where a lot of men died from the camp.
Kept dying.
Meanwhile, they would ship new people, you see, from other communities because they always kept that certain number, and I really couldn't tell you what number.
But eventually, those two camps were joined.
The first camp was Strzelnica, which was the name for a shooting range.
And the second was Majowka.
Eventually, everybody from Strzelnica was sent to Majowka, and that's where I now lived with the two sisters, you see?
And our father was there.
And I was given a job in a laundry.
Yes.
Not with my father.
My father lived in separate barracks with the men.
This camp was run by-- the highest echelon were the Germans-- the Gestapo, the SS, I don't know who-- with the dogs.
And under them were the Ukrainians, in uniform.
No, no.
No uniforms.
Not in there.
Everybody wore clothes, however-- whatever clothes they could scrounge.
There were no-- nothing like Auschwitz type clothes.
No.
No.
We took belongings.
But you see, we were in that camp for close to three years.
So whatever threadbare coat you had, you had to hold on to it for dear life.
And this camp was-- of course there was typhus, and everybody had typhus.
I had typhus.
Later, somebody said you were young enough not to have had a heavy case.
But my oldest sister, Anna, was frightfully ill with typhus, and she-- she was hallucinating.
And we were afraid because the Germans would come and do a selection.
You see, every time there was an epidemic, they would come and tell everybody to-- to line up.
And whoever didn't look right was taken away and shot in a pit.
And there was a selection when my sis-- my older sister, Anna, the one from Canada, she was really hallucinating.
And she was saying things, and she was-- she was burning up with fever.
And we had to prop her up and stand her up for the-- for the [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] whatever it was-- exam.
It wasn't a selection, you know?
They came to see what was cooking, and this out, this out, this out, this out.

And I remember now distinctly that, because I worked in the laundry when I had typhus, and that was one of these-- these line ups again, the women I worked with in the laundry told me to come in and they put me over a steaming cauldron.
Because I was so burning up with fever, you know?
I must have looked like a ghost.
And that saved me.
These women in the laundry saved me, because the steam was over me.
And besides, they just looked in and they saw everybody working.
So they didn't-- they didn't bother to come in and see what's what, because everybody was busy.
No.
No.
There was not.

As far as I remember, if there was anything-- I mean, if you cut-- if anything happened to you-- you cut your hand and you had to bleed and that was the hand you worked with-- maybe they allowed you to have to go and have a kind of a bandage with a little iodine.
Because after all, you know, you couldn't go to work next day if you-- you see?
But basically, whoever was sick or in any way unfit for heavy labor just was-- was totally dismissed as a human being, as a person, as anything.
He didn't count anymore.

So one way or another, he was gotten rid of, whether by shooting by the Ukrainian, or by shooting by the Gestapo, or by-- by-- by being taken out with 20 other men into the woods and being shot there.
And there were-- as a matter of fact, there were a number of very small children smuggled.
And I was a young kid, but there were younger.
There were kids five, six years--
Well, by then I was about 13.
But there were five and six-year-old kids that they even survived Auschwitz, believe it or not, the kids from my home town.
But they didn't survive later, past that.
Past the liberation, they didn't.
But they did survive that camp.
And as I said, the-- the evacuation of-- of-- the liquidation of the town was in October, 1942.
And the camp lasted until the end of June, 1944.
But you see, what happened is that there was a kind of a rebellion.
It wasn't really a rebellion.
But you know, our-- the camp was surrounded with guards and-- in the towers, on the towers.
And very soon-- [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] well, could that-- could have been about July.
Yes.
Could have been July.
The camp at Majowka was totally evacuated to another camp which was nearer to the-- to the-- nearer to the factory and near a siding, near a railroad siding.
This was after.
You see, first was Strzelnica, then was Majowka, then was just Majowka.
Now, in 1944, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] the one camp that was still in existence, about May, let's say, was Majowka.
Now they liquidated Majowka, and they took us to an adjacent-- to a camp that was neither Strzelnica nor Majowka, but another camp that was very near the railroad siding.
And there, of course, they again-- they had the guards and the towers and the dogs and all.
And talk was, nobody went to work anymore.
And talk was that there's going to be-- and I think prob-- we stayed there, probably, only a few days.
But the talk was that we were going to be shipped somewhere.

And the night before-- we didn't know that the next day, whatever that next day was to be-- but on a particular night, what happened to be the night before we were sent out of that last camp, a lot of the young fellows in-- in the-- young fellows and strong women, too, within the camp decided, no more.
And of course, the Germans started shooting.
And there were heaps of people because there were maybe only two openings.
Heaps of people piled up next to the opening.
And I'll never forget.
That night, of course, I-- I-- we didn't escape.
I had--
